summary: This source is a brief description of a BBC Radio 4 podcast episode about Iris Murdoch. It highlights her philosophical stance that morality is an objective fact rather than a subjective matter of taste, and that recognizing good requires seeing the world as it truly is, unclouded by personal desires.

principles.statement: Morality is objective, and good is a fact we can recognize.
principles.rationale: Developed in response to the horrors of the Second World War, countering contemporaries who believed morality was merely subjective or a matter of taste.
principles.source_quote: ...she argued that morality was not subjective or a matter of taste, as many of her contemporaries held, but was objective, and good was a fact we could recognize.

principles.statement: To distinguish good from bad, we must see the world as it really is, not as we want to see it.
principles.rationale: Personal desires and lack of enlightenment obscure our ability to recognize objective good.
principles.source_quote: To tell good from bad, though, we would need to see the world as it really is, not as we want to see it...

anti_patterns.description: Treating morality as subjective or merely a matter of taste.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It denies the objective reality of 'good' as a recognizable fact, a stance she found inadequate after the horrors of the Second World War.

anti_patterns.description: Seeing the world as we want to see it rather than as it really is.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents us from being able to tell good from bad; it is a sign of not being enlightened enough to recognize objective moral facts.
